title: Doppler spectroscopy: from Buys-Ballot's brass band to the wobble of 51 Pegasi
intro: In 1842 a mathematics professor in Prague named Christian Doppler proposed that a moving source compresses its waves on the side it advances toward and stretches them on the side it retreats from. He used the idea to explain (incorrectly, as it turned out) the colors of binary stars. The mistake did not matter; the mechanism survived to be tested on sound three years later by a Dutch meteorologist who hired a railway and a brass band, generalized to light by Fizeau in 1848, and used in 1868 by William Huggins to clock the recession of Sirius from a backyard observatory in south London. Tuned to meters per second by the 1990s, the same fractional shift Δλ/λ = v/c let Michel Mayor and Didier Queloz catch a Jupiter-mass planet pulling 51 Pegasi back and forth on a four-day orbit, and at parts in ten thousand it now runs every redshift survey.

sections.heading: 1. A professor in Prague, and a brass band on a flatcar

blocks.text: Christian Doppler was teaching mathematics at the Prague Polytechnic in 1842, on a salary so meager he was tutoring on the side, when he submitted a memoir to the Royal Bohemian Society titled 'Über das farbige Licht der Doppelsterne und einiger anderer Gestirne des Himmels.' On the colored light of binary stars and certain other heavenly bodies. The thesis: when a wave source moves toward an observer, each successive crest is launched from a position closer to the observer than the last, the crests pile up, and the wavelength shrinks. When the source moves away, the reverse happens, and the wavelength grows. Doppler claimed this was why the components of binary stars often appeared in different colors, one redder and one bluer than its partner.

Doppler was wrong about that specific example. Stellar orbital velocities are a few kilometers per second, the speed of light is three hundred thousand, the fractional shift is one part in a hundred thousand, and the human eye cannot see a color change that small. The colors of double stars come from genuinely different surface temperatures and chemistries, not from the orbital motion of one star around the other. The mechanism, however, was sound; it just needed a test case the eye could actually resolve, and that meant a wave whose speed and frequency were both within reach of nineteenth-century instruments. Sound was within reach. Light was not yet.

Christophorus Henricus Diedericus Buys-Ballot, the Utrecht meteorologist remembered today for the wind-pressure law (winds blow along lines of equal pressure, anticyclones to the right in the northern hemisphere) that bears his name, found the test case. In 1845 he persuaded the Dutch State Railway to lend him an open flatcar on the Utrecht-Maarssen line. He stationed a brass band on the flatcar playing a sustained note, and stationed observers with absolute pitch on the platform with a piano for comparison. The locomotive ran the flatcar back and forth past the platform at about fifty kilometers per hour. The reported pitch rose by roughly a half-tone as the train approached and fell by the same as it receded. Doppler's mechanism worked. Just not, as Doppler had hoped, in the visible colors of binary stars.

sections.heading: 2. Fizeau extends it to light, Huggins reads it off Sirius

blocks.text: In 1848 Hippolyte Fizeau, the French physicist who would later measure the speed of light with a notched toothed wheel and a distant mirror about nine kilometers across the rooftops of Paris, published an independent derivation of the same shift for light. Where Doppler had argued by sound analogy, Fizeau wrote a wavefront-counting argument that worked for any wave with a definite propagation speed. In the small-velocity limit, the two predictions are identical. With λ₀ the rest wavelength of a spectral line, v the radial component of the source's velocity (positive when the source moves away), and c the speed of light, the observed wavelength reads:

blocks.caption: λ_obs is the wavelength of a spectral line as measured by an observer. λ_0 is the rest wavelength of the same line in a stationary laboratory reference. v is the line-of-sight (radial) component of the source's velocity, positive when the source recedes from the observer and negative when it approaches. c is the speed of light in vacuum, 299792458 m/s. Δλ is shorthand for λ_obs minus λ_0, the wavelength shift between the observed and rest line positions. The expression is the first-order, non-relativistic limit valid for v much smaller than c.

blocks.text: The shift is small. For a star receding at 30 km/s (a typical velocity of a nearby Milky Way star relative to the Sun), v/c is 10⁻⁴, and the Hα hydrogen line at a rest wavelength of 656.3 nm moves by 0.066 nm. To read it you need a spectrograph that can compare an observed line position to a laboratory reference to a few parts in ten thousand. Doppler had no such instrument; even Fizeau did not have one calibrated for stellar work. The technology had to wait for William Huggins.

Huggins was a wealthy English silk merchant who sold the business at forty, built an observatory in his back garden in Tulse Hill (a suburb of south London), and turned to spectroscopy. In 1868 he attached a Browning prism spectrograph to his eight-inch refracting telescope, pointed it at Sirius, and measured the position of the F line (Hβ, the second hydrogen Balmer line, at a laboratory wavelength of 486.1 nm) against a laboratory hydrogen tube. The stellar line sat slightly to the red. Huggins read off a recession velocity of 47 km/s and published the result in the Philosophical Transactions. Modern measurements give 12 km/s, so Huggins was off by a factor of four, mostly from imperfect knowledge of the rest wavelength. The principle was sound. Stars had radial velocities, and a spectrograph could read them.

quiz.question: A modern echelle spectrograph (a coarsely ruled grating used in high diffraction orders to deliver high spectral resolution in a compact instrument) achieves a wavelength precision of Δλ ≈ 0.001 nm at the Hα hydrogen line (rest wavelength 656.3 nm). For radial velocities much smaller than the speed of light, what is the smallest line-of-sight velocity this precision can resolve?
options: About 30 km/s, the typical stellar velocity Huggins was targeting in 1868.

About 460 m/s. Setting Δλ/λ = v/c with Δλ = 0.001 nm, λ = 656.3 nm, and c = 3 × 10⁵ km/s gives v = c × (0.001/656.3) ≈ 0.457 km/s, which is roughly the precision of the radial-velocity spectrographs of the late 1980s.
About 1 m/s, the precision of HARPS (the High Accuracy Radial-velocity Planet Searcher commissioned at La Silla in 2003) that detected the first super-Earth-mass planets.
About 0.001 km/s directly; in the small-velocity limit, a wavelength precision in nm converts to a velocity precision in km/s with no further calculation.
quiz.explanation: Option 0 confuses what is being measured (the velocity precision) with what Huggins was aiming at (a 30 km/s recession). Option 2 quotes the right ballpark for the best modern instruments (HARPS and ESPRESSO operate at 0.1 to 1 m/s) but those use a wavelength precision of about 10⁻⁵ nm, not 10⁻³ nm. Option 3 invents a unit identification with no physical content. The honest answer is option 1, the direct application of Δλ/λ = v/c. A fractional precision of 0.001/656.3 ≈ 1.5 × 10⁻⁶ corresponds to v = c × 1.5 × 10⁻⁶ ≈ 460 m/s. That figure is roughly the precision of the radial-velocity spectrographs of the late 1980s and was sufficient for detecting substellar companions and large-amplitude pulsations, but it was not enough for any planet smaller than Jupiter, which is why the first exoplanet detection had to wait for ELODIE in 1995.

sections.heading: 3. Why the formula works only when the source is slow

blocks.text: The wave-counting derivation makes the formula intuitive. A source at rest emits one crest every period T_0. A source moving toward the observer with velocity v emits each new crest from a position closer to the observer by v × T_0 than the previous crest. The wavelength as measured by the observer is therefore λ_0 minus v T_0, equivalently λ_0 (1 minus v/c). The argument is one paragraph long, requires no field theory, and gives the right answer whenever v is much smaller than c.

The argument has a hidden assumption: that the source's clock and the observer's clock tick at the same rate. For sound this is harmless, since the source and the observer share the air as a medium and a common notion of time. For light there is no medium (the Michelson-Morley experiment of 1887, an interferometer that compared the speed of light along two perpendicular arms across a fringe pattern, ruled out a luminiferous ether to one part in 10⁹), and a moving source's clock runs slower by the relativistic factor γ = 1/√(1 − β²), with β = v/c. The full Doppler formula, derived by Einstein in 1905, picks this up:

blocks.caption: f_obs is the frequency the observer measures from the light of a receding source. f_source is the frequency the source measures in its own rest frame. v is the radial velocity, positive for recession. β is v divided by c, positive for recession and negative for approach. c is the speed of light in vacuum. The square-root factor is the exact result of relativistic time dilation combined with the wavefront-counting argument. For β much smaller than 1, expanding the square root gives 1 minus β plus β² over 2 minus higher orders, recovering the Galilean Δλ/λ = v/c at first order.

blocks.text: At β = 0.01 (3000 km/s, the radial velocity of a typical galaxy in the local volume) the relativistic and Galilean formulas agree to one part in 10⁴. At β = 0.5 (the regime of quasar jets) they diverge by 30 percent. There is also a second-order shift, the transverse Doppler effect, the time-dilation contribution that survives even when the source moves perpendicular to the line of sight, and the Galilean formula predicts zero shift. It is purely a γ-factor effect, of order (v/c)², measured first in 1938 by Herbert Ives and G.R. Stilwell in a beam of hydrogen ions (a discharge tube produces such ions as 'canal rays' accelerated through a few thousand volts) at the Bell Telephone Laboratories.

For the rest of this lesson the radial velocities are tens of meters per second up to a few hundred kilometers per second, the simple Δλ/λ = v/c is the only formula needed, and the relativistic correction at v = 100 km/s is one part in 10⁷, smaller than any spectrograph can measure. Exoplanet hunters use the Galilean formula. Cosmologists, when they push the redshift z above one, switch to the general-relativity formula 1 + z = a_now/a_emit, the ratio of cosmic scale factors at the present time and at the time of emission, which is not a velocity formula at all.

sections.heading: 4. The wobble: how a star betrays its planet

blocks.text: Newton's third law applies to a planet orbiting a star just as it does to a swung mass on a string. The planet pulls on the star with the same force the star pulls on the planet. What orbits the center of mass of the system is both of them, the planet in a large ellipse and the star in a tiny one scaled down by the mass ratio M_p divided by M_*. Seen edge-on from across light-years, the stellar orbit is too small to resolve as an angular displacement on the sky, but the line-of-sight component of the star's motion rises and falls in a sinusoidal Doppler shift of the spectral lines. That is the radial-velocity method.

For a circular orbit seen edge-on (with inclination angle i = 90°, so sin i = 1) and with M_p much smaller than M_*, the half-amplitude of the stellar radial-velocity sine wave is, to leading order in the mass ratio:

blocks.caption: K is the half-amplitude of the stellar radial-velocity sine wave, the peak deviation from the system's mean velocity that the spectrograph reads off in meters per second. M_p is the mass of the planet. sin i is the sine of the orbital inclination angle, where i = 90° is an edge-on orbit (sin i = 1) and i = 0° is a face-on orbit (sin i = 0 and no radial signal at all). M_J is the mass of Jupiter, 1.898 × 10²⁷ kg, used as a convenient mass unit. P is the orbital period of the planet around the star. M_⊙ is the mass of the Sun. The constant 28.4 m/s absorbs Newton's gravitational constant G and the unit conversions. The combination M_p sin i is what the radial-velocity method directly measures, and the inclination i is degenerate with planet mass unless an independent observation breaks the degeneracy.

blocks.text: Michel Mayor and Didier Queloz at the Haute-Provence Observatory in southern France built ELODIE, a fiber-fed echelle spectrograph stable to about 13 m/s. In October 1995 they announced a Doppler signal in 51 Pegasi, a quiet Sun-like G2V star fifty light-years away in the constellation Pegasus: a K = 56 m/s sine wave with a period of 4.231 days. Plug the numbers into the formula: M_p sin i ≈ 0.47 M_J. A roughly half-Jupiter-mass planet orbiting at 0.05 AU, eight times closer to its star than Mercury sits to the Sun. The result shocked the planet-formation community, which had predicted gas giants only at Jupiter-like distances or further, and forced the invention of orbital migration as a mainstream theory of giant-planet assembly.

blocks.caption: The reflex radial velocity of a Sun-mass star pulled by a planet of mass M_p on a circular, edge-on orbit of period P, drawn over one orbital cycle. Drag the M_p slider (logarithmic across 0.001 to 10 Jupiter masses, i.e. about 0.3 Earth masses up to 10 Jupiters) and the P slider (1 to 1000 days) and watch the K amplitude breathe. The dashed horizontal lines mark three eras of radial-velocity precision: ELODIE 1995 at about 13 m/s, HARPS 2003 at about 1 m/s, ESPRESSO 2018 at about 10 cm/s. The three preset buttons snap to historical reference orbits: 51 Pegasi b with K = 56 m/s, Jupiter around the Sun with K = 12.5 m/s, the Earth around the Sun with K = 9 cm/s.

blocks.text: Twenty-five years later the radial-velocity floor has dropped to about 30 cm/s with ESPRESSO at the VLT (the Very Large Telescope, the European Southern Observatory's array of four 8-meter telescopes in northern Chile), and the technique has found a few thousand planets. The Earth around the Sun produces a K of nine centimeters per second, still below current detection limits; reaching it is the engineering target of the next generation of stabilized spectrographs.

quiz.question: The radial-velocity method has a known geometric peculiarity: it preferentially detects planets that are massive, close to their stars, and viewed edge-on. Which of these three biases is structural (built into the method's geometry rather than into instrument sensitivity), and what physical quantity does the method actually measure for an arbitrarily inclined orbit?
options: The bias toward massive planets is structural; the method measures M_p directly, with the inclination affecting only whether the planet transits across the stellar disk.

The bias toward edge-on orbits is structural; the method measures the combination M_p sin i, the product of planet mass and the sine of the orbital inclination angle. A face-on orbit (i = 0°) gives sin i = 0 and no radial signal at any sensitivity, while an edge-on orbit (i = 90°) gives the full M_p. Without an independent inclination measurement from a transit, an astrometric wobble across the sky, or a direct image, the method delivers a minimum mass, not the true mass.
The bias toward close-in (short-period) orbits is structural; the radial-velocity method cannot detect any planet outside 0.5 AU at any sensitivity, regardless of stellar mass.

All three biases are equally structural; the method has no geometric leverage at all and measures whatever line-of-sight combination of M_p, sin i, P, and orbital eccentricity the orbit happens to present.
quiz.explanation: Option 0 swaps two of the biases: instrument sensitivity does favor massive planets, but the mass-vs-inclination degeneracy is what is built into the geometry, and the method does not measure M_p alone. Option 2 turns a sensitivity preference into a hard cutoff; the K formula falls as P^(-1/3), so short-period planets are easier but not exclusively detectable, and at large period the trade goes against you slowly. Option 3 dissolves the question by treating all biases as identical. The honest answer is option 1. The radial-velocity method measures only the line-of-sight component of the star's reflex motion, so the inclination angle enters as a multiplicative sin i in the amplitude. What is read off the spectrograph is the combination M_p sin i, and a face-on orbit produces zero signal at any sensitivity, regardless of planet mass. The inclination degeneracy can be lifted by combining a radial-velocity detection with a transit (which constrains i ≈ 90° because the planet must cross the stellar disk), with astrometric astrometry (which measures the orthogonal component), or with a direct image. Until then, every M_p reported in a radial-velocity catalog should be read as a minimum mass.

sections.heading: 5. One ruler, 1.3 million galaxies: BOSS reads the BAO at z = 0.57

blocks.text: In November 2014 the Baryon Oscillation Spectroscopic Survey (BOSS), the 2009 to 2014 program of the Sloan Digital Sky Survey using the 2.5-meter telescope at Apache Point Observatory in southern New Mexico, published a measurement of the cosmic distance scale at redshift z = 0.57 with a precision of one percent. The instrument was a pair of spectrographs fed by a thousand optical fibers, each one plugged by hand at the start of each night into a hole drilled in an aluminum plate placed at the telescope's focal plane, each one carrying the light of a single distant galaxy down a thirty-meter run of fiber to the spectrograph in the basement. The data product, after a decade of operation, was a catalog of 1.3 million galaxy redshifts.

What each fiber recorded was a row of emission and absorption lines: the [O II] doublet at a rest wavelength of 372.7 nm, the Ca II H and K stellar lines at 393.4 and 396.8 nm, and the 4000-angstrom break (a discontinuity at 400 nm produced by the cumulative absorption of metals in evolved stellar populations). Each feature in the observed spectrum sat shifted to longer wavelength by the same fractional factor 1 + z. The value of z was read off by least-squares-fitting the observed line positions against laboratory rest wavelengths. For BOSS's targets at z ≈ 0.5, the wavelength has been stretched by the expansion of space itself, not by a local velocity, but the operational measurement is the same line-fit that Huggins performed on Sirius in 1868.

The cosmological measurement asked a specific question: at what physical separation, in this redshift slice, do galaxy pairs cluster more often than random expectation predicts? The answer was a 1 percent excess at a comoving separation of about 150 megaparsecs, the BAO scale (baryon acoustic oscillation, the wavelength of pressure waves frozen into the matter distribution at recombination at z ≈ 1100, when the photon-baryon plasma stopped supporting acoustic modes). The same 150-megaparsec ruler, stretched by cosmic expansion to its proper size at z = 0.57 and then read off the galaxy distribution at that redshift, is recovered to one-percent precision. The dark-energy density that drives the expansion comes out of the same measurement, by anchoring the cosmological model against the ruler at two different redshifts.

Each of those 1.3 million redshifts is Huggins's Sirius measurement done over again, by silicon. Huggins read one line in one spectrum of one star, by candlelight, and reported 47 km/s with errors he could not yet calibrate. BOSS performs the same line fit, twenty lines in each of a million spectra, in a pipeline, and reports a one-percent measurement of a length scale older than every galaxy in the survey. The fitted line is the same fit. The instrument has been lapped by six orders of magnitude in throughput and four orders in wavelength precision in the century and a half since.

finalQuiz.question: A new optical spectrograph delivers a Doppler-velocity precision of about 1 m/s. A team uses it for two distinct programs: a long-baseline radial-velocity search for an Earth-mass planet around a Sun-like star at four parsecs, and a redshift survey targeting Lyman-α emitting galaxies at z ≈ 3 to refine the BAO standard ruler. Across both programs the instrument records the same operational quantity, the fractional spectral shift Δλ/λ, but the physical content extracted is very different. Why?
options: The two programs read off different combinations of orbital parameters and cosmic distances, but in both cases Δλ/λ = v/c gives a meaningful line-of-sight velocity; at z = 3 the inferred velocity is 9 × 10⁸ m/s, or three times the speed of light, which is the correct answer for that redshift.

For the exoplanet program, Δλ/λ measures a roughly 9 cm/s line-of-sight reflex velocity of the host star caused by Newtonian planetary motion, and the Doppler formula Δλ/λ = v/c gives v directly. For the redshift survey at z = 3, Δλ/λ instead measures the ratio a_now/a_emit minus 1, the cumulative stretch of the photon's wavelength by the expansion of space between emission and observation. The relevant scale-factor ratio at z = 3 is 4, the Galilean Doppler 'velocity' would exceed c well below z = 1, and the right formula is the general-relativistic 1 + z = a_now/a_emit. Both programs are calibrated by the same laboratory rest wavelengths; the BAO scale at the target redshift is then recovered from the geometric ratio of comoving distance to the sound-horizon ruler, not by inverting v/c.
Both programs measure a radial velocity v in m/s, and the BAO scale at z = 3 is recovered by multiplying v by the look-back time in seconds to convert the velocity into a distance.

The exoplanet program measures only the planet's mass, and the redshift program measures only the universe's age; the two programs read off the same Δλ/λ but invert different functions of it, with no reference to either velocity or geometry.
finalQuiz.explanation: Option 0 mistakes a v/c calculation that produces v > c for evidence of a valid velocity inference; it instead signals that v/c is the wrong formula at that redshift. Option 2 invents a multiplication with no physical content. Option 3 splits the two programs into mutually exclusive quantity readouts they were never mutually exclusive about. The honest answer is option 1, and it sits at the conceptual seam between local kinematics and cosmological geometry. The fractional spectral shift Δλ/λ is the operational measurement a spectrograph delivers: identify a line, compare to a laboratory reference. Its physical interpretation depends entirely on what produced the shift. For v much smaller than c, in the local volume, the shift is a Doppler effect and Δλ/λ = v/c reads off a line-of-sight velocity directly. At cosmological redshifts beyond about z = 0.01 the wavelength has been stretched by the expansion of space itself, the right formula is 1 + z = a_now/a_emit (the ratio of cosmic scale factors at the present time and at the time of emission), and modern surveys use z as a coordinate, not as a velocity. The same fraction Δλ/λ supports two distinct physical extractions because two distinct theories tie it to physics.
